segments.text:
Hepatitis A
CLS-N390 Vaccines: Science and Society

Hepatitis A
Single-stranded RNA
N o envelope (naked)
V ery small
Picornavius family
E nteroviruses (enteric diseases), rhinoviruses (colds)
Found worldwide
Humans are only reservior

Transmission
Highly contagious
Transmissible 2 weeks before illness to 1 week after onset of jaundice
Fecal-oral route – infected individuals shed virus in feces
Close person-to-person contact
Poor hand hygiene
Contaminated food or water
Sexual contact
H 0 = estimated at 2.1 – 2.8

At Risk Population
Chronic liver disease
HIV
Increased risk
International travelers
Men who have sex with men
Drug users (injection and non-injection)
Occupational risk for exposure
Close personal content with an international adoptee
Homeless
Risk of severe disease

Symptoms
Incubation period – 28 days (range of 15-50 days)
Acute hepatitis A is indistinguishable from other forms of acute hepatitis
Fever
Fatigue
Loss of appetite
Nausea
Vomiting
Abdominal pain
Dark urine
Diarrhea
Clay-colored stool
Joint pain
Jaundice
Illness doesn’t last longer than two months
10% - 15% have prolonged or relapsing signs and symptoms for up to six months

Characteristics of Vaccine
Inactivated whole-virus vaccines
Inactivated with formalin
Adsorbed to aluminum hydroxide adjuvant
Cell culture in human fibroblasts
2 dose series –
pediatric = 12 months through 18 years; adult = 19+

Hepatitis A Vaccines
HAVRIX
(GlaxoSmithKline)
Preservative – 2-phenoxyethanol
Pediatric and adult doses
2 nd dose 6-12 months after first
Efficacy – 94%
VAQTA
(Merk)
No preservative
Pediatric and adult doses
2 nd dose 6-18 months after first
Efficacy – 100%

History of Hepatitis A
Prior to 2004 – hepatitis A was the most frequently reported type of hepatitis in United States
